narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 53% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 school decile of 3 and Year 3 decile of 2—both well below the typical student's averages of about 5.7 and 5.5—along with your LSAT score of 25.5, undergraduate GPA of 3.1, middle family income bracket, and other details like being a full-time female White student. Among these, your Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest influence, pushing the model toward predicting failure because it signals consistently lower performance compared to peers. Your LSAT score came in second, as its lower-than-average value further reinforced concerns about bar exam readiness. Your Year 1 decile ranked third in impact, similarly highlighting early academic struggles relative to most applicants. Factors like family income, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status had progressively smaller influences, all nudging the prediction slightly toward failure but not as decisively. Overall, your profile stands out for weaker academic deciles and LSAT compared to the dataset's stronger averages, where most students who pass have higher marks across the board. While demographics like being White and female align closely with common successful profiles (83.8% White, 43.6% female), they couldn't offset the academic signals. To improve your future chances, focus on boosting your academic deciles through better study habits and coursework, as those were the top drivers here and are within your control. Retaking the LSAT to aim above the 36.7 average could make a big difference, given its high ranking. Strengthening your undergraduate-level performance or equivalent preparation might also help tip the scales. With targeted effort on these realistic areas, you could shift the prediction toward passing and strengthen your next application. Keep pushing forward—you have the potential to turn this around.
